llm_judge_candidates.truth_value: Oxide map (Fig. 3) at 800°C showing four composition regions based on scale morphology: Region I (low Al) – alumina with large/small oxide nodules; Region II (duplex α+γ) – alumina with bulky Mn‑rich scales and oxide threads; Region III (ferritic, optimum 5–10% Mn, 6–10% Al) – continuous protective alumina with small protrusions; Region IV (Al >9%) – alumina with small Mn‑rich nodules and spallation.
llm_judge_candidates.prediction_value: Kinetics: Log-log plots of weight gain versus time (Fig. 1) show breakaway and healing behavior for non-protective alloys and near-parabolic behavior for protective alumina formers. The rate equation (ΔW/A)^n = kt yielded a large range of n values, indicating few alloys obeyed simple rate equations; ΔW/24 values were used for comparison of oxidation rates.

llm_judge_candidates.truth_value: Manganese addition: <2% Mn drastically increases oxidation rate; 5–10% Mn reduces rate by replacing large iron oxide nodules with smaller Mn‑rich nodules; >10% Mn promotes austenite formation and bulky manganese oxides that degrade oxidation resistance.
llm_judge_candidates.prediction_value: Table I (24 hr at 800°C): Weight gains of Fe-Mn-Al alloys span from approximately 0.021 mg cm^-2 (Fe-9Mn-4Al) to over 1 mg cm^-2 (Fe-20Mn-6Al, 18 hr). Minimum weight gains occur in the composition range Fe-(5–10)%Mn-(6–10)%Al (e.g., Fe-5Mn-7Al: 0.033; Fe-10Mn-6Al: 0.054; Fe-5Mn-9Al: 0.021 mg cm^-2). Additions of less than 2% Mn greatly increased oxidation rate of binary Fe-Al alloys, whereas 5–10% Mn significantly reduced it.

llm_judge_candidates.truth_value: Austenite is detrimental: it promotes breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and growth of bulky Mn‑rich oxides. Totally ferritic alloys necessary for good oxidation resistance.

llm_judge_candidates.truth_value: Small chromium additions (e.g., 12% Cr in Fe‑10Mn‑4Al‑12Cr) enable protective alumina scale formation at lower Al contents. Alloys with less than 5% Al require high Cr to avoid breakaway.

Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr showed highest 24h gain 2.663 mg cm^-2. Fe-5Mn-4Al-5Cr data 0.292 mg cm^-2 corresponds to 15 hr oxidation.

llm_judge_candidates.truth_value: Nodule growth mechanism in Region IV involves void formation beneath alumina scale, Mn vapor transport through voids, reaction with oxygen to form Mn2O3, and subsequent filling of voids with Mn oxide followed by Al healing layer.

Type 3 (≥7.5% Mn) small Mn-rich nodules with continuous Mn2O3 outer layer and complete healing layer of Al2O3/aluminate at base; absence of (Fe,Mn)3O4 due to continuous aluminate.

llm_judge_candidates.truth_value: Healing layers of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the metal‑oxide interface can stop growth of large nodules and cause transition to protective behavior. Incomplete isolation of (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer leads to enhanced Fe2O3 formation.
